data.title: The Week That Was, The Week Ahead: Macro and Markets, Feb. 15
data.description:
U.S. stocks ended the week lower as investors moved away from big tech and focused more on safer areas of the market. The S&P 500 (SPX) fell 1.28%, the Nasdaq 100 (NDX) dropped 1.27%, and the Dow (DJIA) slid 1.15%. At the same time, the 10-year Treasury yield eased to about 4.09%, while gold (CM:XAUUSD) jumped above $5,000 and oil (CM:CL) held near $63 a barrel.

data.title: 'Not Just Yet,' Says Top Investor About Oracle Stock
data.description:
The news of the incredible surge in backlog for Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) last fall sent the stock soaring by 36% in one day alone. Reporting remaining performance obligations of $455 billion (up a whopping 360% year-over-year) can apparently have that effect.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Since that spike, however, more than five months ago, it's been a mostly downhill ride for Oracle. All told, ORCL has seen its share price cut in half since mid-September.

The reasons are both broad-based and specific to Oracle. Fears of an AI bubble have been popping up, as are worries that the company will be forced to burden its balance sheet with massive amounts of debt in order to build out the required infrastructure.

Top investor Will Ebiefung spots another reason to be concerned: overexposure to OpenAI.

"While OpenAI remains a leader in generative artificial intelligence, it is an extremely risky partner to build a business around," explains the 5-star investor, who is among the top 3% of stock pros covered by TipRanks.

Ebiefung points out that over half of Oracle's contractual backlog is tied to OpenAI. The Financial Times has even gone as far as to deem Oracle a publicly-traded proxy for OpenAI.

It's easy to see how this arrangement will benefit Oracle, acknowledges Ebiefung, as it will be providing the necessary infrastructure for the vast compute power required for OpenAI's LLMs.

And yet, he also notes that OpenAI is still churning through money, citing a report that estimates the company could burn up to $115 billion in cash by 2029. It's the push and pull of all this spending that stands at the crux of the investment argument for and against AI, according to the investor.

"Investors are essentially betting that the technology will continue to improve at a rate that justifies the huge levels of capital spending and debt Oracle is pouring into data centers for its clients," adds Ebiefung.

For that reason, the investor isn't looking at ORCL's dip as a buying opportunity, preferring to wait on the sidelines until it falls even lower.

"Shares could get even cheaper as these long-term challenges play out," concludes Ebiefung. (To watch Will Ebiefung's track record, click here)

Wall Street is a bit more optimistic, however. With 26 Buys and 7 Holds, ORCL enjoys a Strong Buy consensus rating. Its 12-month average price target of $289.62 points to gains of 80% in the year ahead. (See ORCL stock forecast)
data.author: Gabe Ross

data.title: 'Just Getting Hammered,' Says Top Investor About Palantir Stock
data.description:
Quarterly earnings reports and their accompanying calls present an opportunity for company management to trumpet successes, explain shortcomings, and outline future plans.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Alex Karp, the CEO of Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR), seems to relish this platform. His letters to shareholders, published online, are sprinkled with literary references, historical parallels, and contemporary quotes.

And, of course, lately, they have been full of very strong words of celebration for Palantir's incredible performance. In his most recent musing, published earlier this month in conjunction with Palantir's Q4 and full-year earnings report, Karp called the company's recent record-breaking profit "pure and uncontrived." With surging top- and bottom-line growth, Karp has plenty of reasons to feel exuberant.

And yet, the market hasn't been quite as rewarding, and it has pushed Palantir's share price down by 11% since its Q4 earnings report just a few short weeks ago. Though top investor Daniel Sparks certainly appreciates Palantir's might, he just isn't ready to dive on in.

"The stock is simply priced for perfection, leaving essentially no room for error," explains the 5-star investor, who is among the top 1% of stock pros covered by TipRanks. "One of Wall Street's darlings of the last few years is getting hammered."

The investor points out that Palantir's valuation is off the charts, with a price-to-earnings ratio north of 200x. He also notes that its market capitalization of more than $306 billion pales in comparison to its trailing twelve-month sales and net income of approximately $4.5 billion and $1.6 billion, respectively.

"The gap between Palantir's market capitalization and its underlying fundamentals is a chasm," adds Sparks.

The investor further details that even a little slowdown could trigger a "disaster." Though Sparks isn't expecting that to occur anytime soon, he also cites a deceleration of its total contract value growth (from 151% in Q3 to 138% in Q4) as one potential crack in the growth story.

(He does hasten to add that 138% remains quite an impressive figure.)

The big risk in not buying into Palantir is that the company "continues to fire on all cylinders," says Sparks, who emphasizes that this really is a "great business." However, there's a price for everything, and this is one that Sparks isn't willing to pay.

"While it's always possible that Palantir delivers the extraordinary growth required to justify its valuation and still reward shareholders with attractive returns from here, I don't think the risk is worth it at the stock's current price," concludes Sparks. (To watch Daniel Sparks' track record, click here)

Wall Street, on the other hand, is feeling pretty comfortable with PLTR's price, at least for now. With 11 Buys, 6 Holds, and 2 Sells, PLTR enjoys a Moderate Buy consensus rating. Its 12-month average price target of $192.38 implies an upside of ~46%. (See PLTR stock forecast)
data.author: Gabe Ross

data.title: Bank of America (BAC) Raises CEO Moynihan's Compensation 17% to $41 Million
data.description:
Bank of America (BAC) CEO Brian Moynihan has gotten a hefty pay raise.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Moynihan's compensation for 2025 rose 17% year-over-year to $41 million. Bank of America's board of directors maintained Moynihan's base salary at $1.5 million with no cash bonus, while granting equity incentive awards totaling $39.5 million, according to a regulatory filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

The compensation increase follows a year in which Bank of America, the second-largest U.S. bank, improved its financial performance with net income rising 13% to $30.5 billion and earnings per share (EPS) increasing 19% to $3.81. Revenue last year grew 7% to $113.1 billion, reflecting record net interest income at the financial institution.

Bank of America's Stock Performance

In raising Moynihan's compensation, Bank of America's board said that his leadership helped drive shareholder growth in 2025. BAC stock rose 25% in 2025, reaching a new all-time high during the year. This followed a 31% increase in 2024. The company's market capitalization increased 19% last year, maintaining its position as the second-largest U.S. bank by market value after JPMorgan Chase (JPM).

Moynihan's compensation includes restricted stock options that vest over 12 months, and stock options that vest over four years. Under Moynihan, Bank of America maintains a strong balance sheet with total assets of $3.4 trillion. Its loan balances grew 8% and its deposits increased 3% in 2025.

Is BAC Stock a Buy?

Bank of America's stock has a consensus Strong Buy rating among 19 Wall Street analysts. That rating is based on 15 Buy and four Hold recommendations issued in the last three months. The average BAC price target of $61.64 implies 17.59% upside from current levels.
data.author: Joel Baglole

data.title: CoreWeave (CRWV) Lawsuit Adds Pressure Before Earnings as AI Capacity Questions Mount
data.description:
CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) faces a new legal chapter of uncertainty after a securities class action lawsuit was filed in a U.S. court in New Jersey. The suit claims the AI cloud firm misled investors about its ability to scale its data center buildout and meet strong demand for its services. The news comes three days before CoreWeave reports its fourth-quarter and end-of-the-year results on Wednesday.

President's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The case centers on claims that CoreWeave overstated its ability to deploy AI systems "at massive scale" and to "rapidly scale our operations." In addition, the complaint alleges that the company failed to disclose key delays tied to third-party data center partners. Investors have until March 13, 2026, to seek lead plaintiff status in the case.

Meanwhile, CRWV shares rose slightly on Friday, closing at $96.04.
data.author: Ran Melamed

data.title: 'Built for the Agent-Era' China's AI Giants Roll Out New Models, Putting Pressure on NVDA and GOOGL
data.description:
China's AI sector is showing a fresh wave of new models as the country heads into the Spring Festival season. The timing is notable, since it has been one year since DeepSeek's breakout work helped shift the global AI scene. Now, major firms are moving fast again, with new tools in video, robotics, and large language models.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

At the same time, several Chinese tech giants are offering close to $700 million in cash gifts and rewards, using the holiday season to boost app use and market share.

Video and Robotics Take the Lead

One of the biggest themes in this new wave is video AI. ByteDance, the parent of TikTok, launched Seedance 2.0 on February 12. The company said the model can take text, image, audio, and video input at once, and it can create a 2K video with sound that stays in sync.

Soon after, ByteDance also released Doubao 2.0, which, as reported, was built for the "agent era." That points to a push toward AI tools that can act more on their own, rather than just respond to prompts.

Meanwhile, Kuaishou Technology (KUASF) launched Kling AI 3.0, which can generate video and audio together. The company said the model now supports clips up to 15 seconds long and includes a Multi Shot feature that adjusts camera angles on its own. Kuaishou noted that Kling has already served over 60 million creators and produced more than 600 million videos.

Meanwhile, Alibaba Group (BABA) is also expanding beyond chatbots. Through its DAMO Academy, the company released RynnBrain, a robotics AI model built for real-world tasks. Bloomberg reported the model can help machines move through cluttered spaces and resume work after stops, using memory and spatial skills.

Language Models and a Holiday User Push

Alongside video and robotics, China's language model race is still active. Zhipu AI released its flagship GLM 5 model on February 11. The firm said the model reaches top scores in coding and agent work.

Shanghai-based MiniMax (HK:0100) also launched M2.5 on February 12. The company claimed it can match Claude Opus' level results while costing "one twentieth of the price."

At the same time, these launches are tied to heavy holiday promo plans. Tencent Holdings (TCEHY), Baidu (BIDU), Alibaba, and ByteDance are offering large hongbao-style cash gifts to win users. Alibaba's Qwen App alone launched a 3-billion-yuan plan, or about $431 million, which briefly pushed it to the top of China's App Store ranks.

Bottom Line

China's latest AI wave shows that competition is no longer limited to U.S. firms like Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), or OpenAI. Instead, China's largest players are building their own fast-moving AI market.

We used TipRanks' Comparison Tool to assemble all the tickers mentioned in the piece. This is a useful tool to gain an in-depth view of each stock and the broader AI industry. This includes both the American and the Chinese AI industries.
data.author: Ran Melamed

data.title: 'No Force on Earth Could Stop the Fallout,' Anthropic CEO Warns Trillion-Dollar AI Spending
data.description:
Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei warns that the AI boom may be moving too fast in spending. In a new podcast interview, he said some rivals are taking big risks on data center build-out without fully grasping the math behind it.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

"I get the impression that some of the other companies have not written down the spreadsheet, that they don't really understand the risks they're taking," Amodei said on the Dwarkesh Podcast. He added that some firms are "just doing stuff because it sounds cool."

His remarks come as AI leaders plan to invest heavily in chips, power, and new sites to train and run models.

Infrastructure Bets Are Getting Bigger

At the center of the debate is OpenAI. Last year, OpenAI outlined plans that could total about $1.4 trillion to build up to 30 gigawatts of compute power over time. Chief Executive Sam Altman has also spoken about adding one gigawatt a week at full scale.

Amodei did not name OpenAI, but he described a risk that fits such plans. "If you're buying a trillion dollars a year of compute for 2027 because you think you're going to make a trillion dollars in revenue, and instead you make $800 billion, no force on earth could stop the fallout," he said.

In simple terms, he is pointing to a mismatch risk. Data centers take one to two years to build. Power deals must be locked in early. If revenue falls short even by 20%, the fixed costs remain. That gap can strain cash flow and debt.

This matters for public firms tied to the AI buildout. Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) sells the chips at the core of these data centers. Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Amazon (AMZN) are all pouring billions into AI cloud gear. If demand stays strong, those bets may pay off. However, if growth slows, the market could reassess the actual capacity needed.

Enterprise Focus Versus Consumer Scale

At the same time, Anthropic is not standing still. Reports suggest it aims to secure at least 10 gigawatts of compute over the next few years, which could cost hundreds of billions of dollars.

Still, Amodei framed his firm as more measured. "We've thought carefully about it. We're an enterprise business. Therefore, we can rely more on revenue. It's less fickle than consumer demand," he said.

In addition, Anthropic said it will pay for 100% of grid upgrades tied to its sites and cover any rise in power costs for local users. That move may ease pushback and speed up projects, which have become more complex as power demand rises.

For investors, the broader issue is not whether AI works. Most leaders in the field, including Amodei, still see rapid progress ahead. The real question is timing. Can revenue scale fast enough to match the scale of new build plans?

We used TipRanks' Comparison Tool to compare notable publicly traded companies that employ chatbots similar to ChatGPT, Grok, and Claude. It's an excellent tool to gain a broader perspective on each stock and the AI industry as a whole.
data.author: Ran Melamed

data.title: AI Video Arms Race Expands as ByteDance Enters the Field, Raising Stakes for NVDA and GOOGL
data.description:
ByteDance, the parent company of TikTok, has launched Seedance 2.0, a new AI model that generates a short video from a single text prompt, with a clear story, voice, and sound. The clips run up to 15 seconds and will soon be available to global users through CapCut, which has 642 million monthly active users.

Valentine's Day Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The release places ByteDance in direct competition with OpenAI and Alphabet (GOOGL), which offer their own text-to-video tools. However, its distribution seems to be the bigger story.

The TikTok Playbook Moves to AI Video

Seedance 2.0 can create scene changes, voice-overs, and character motion from a simple prompt. A film director who tested the tool said, "Storyboarding has long been considered the one area where human thinking and innovation really shone. It's now been proven that AI can replace it."

That statement highlights how fast AI video tools are improving. At the same time, Seedance will be built into CapCut, which already sits at the center of short-form video creation. This gives ByteDance a built-in global base, rather than relying on a new platform.

In contrast, OpenAI's Sora is still limited to select users. Google's Veo is tied to YouTube and cloud tools. ByteDance is placing AI video inside an app that creators already use daily. As a result, the adoption curve could move faster.

Compute Demand and Legal Risk

There is also a hardware angle. Video generation requires heavy computing power. ByteDance is nearing a deal to access more than 7,000 Nvidia (NVDA), Blackwell B200 chips in Indonesia, according to people familiar with the matter. Those chips, made by Nvidia, cannot be shipped directly to China due to U.S. export controls. Still, the move shows how AI video could add to long-term demand for advanced chips.

Meanwhile, legal concerns are building. The Motion Picture Association said the model used U.S. copyrighted works without approval "on a massive scale." In addition, a filmmaker claimed the tool produced audio that sounded nearly identical to his voice, despite not using a voice sample. ByteDance later suspended a feature that allowed the creation of digital avatars based on real people.

In a broader perspective, AI video could disrupt content production and editing software. However, copyright claims and privacy risk may slow global rollout, especially in the U.S.

Overall, Seedance 2.0 signals that AI video is becoming a core battleground. ByteDance may not lead in chat tools, but in short-form video creation, it is building on a base that already spans hundreds of millions of users.

We used TipRanks' Comparison Tool to assemble notable AI firms that employ an AI video generator. This is a useful tool to gain an in-depth view of each stock and the broader industry.
data.author: Ran Melamed
